The deal represents the first concrete product from Spotify's October 2025 partnership with major labels—UMG, Sony Music Group, Warner Music Group, Merlin, and Believe—to develop AI music tools with explicit consent and compensation frameworks.

## First Label Partnership Materializes

Spotify has not disclosed launch timing or pricing beyond the Premium tier. The company framed the initiative around three principles: artist choice in participation, fair compensation through royalty mechanisms, and strengthened artist-fan relationships. Sir Lucian Grainge, UMG's CEO, positioned the tool as a way to "support human artistry" while creating additional revenue streams for rights holders. Alex Norström, Spotify's co-CEO, emphasized that the system is "grounded in consent, credit, and compensation."

The move follows months of tension between streaming platforms and record labels over AI training data and generative music. This agreement sidesteps some of that friction by establishing a licensing model upfront rather than seeking forgiveness later. The royalty pass-through to artists distinguishes this from some competitor offerings that have faced legal challenges.

## Licensing Model Tests Enterprise Viability

The deal signals a potential market for AI music tools that operate within existing copyright frameworks rather than circumventing them. If Spotify successfully scales the product and demonstrates that users will pay for remix functionality, it could establish a template for other platforms negotiating with rights holders. Conversely, if adoption remains limited, it may indicate that AI music generation holds value primarily as an internal product feature rather than a standalone offering.

For enterprise AI vendors working in regulated creative industries, the model underscores that consent-based frameworks and transparent compensation can be viable—though they require negotiation with entrenched stakeholders. The success or failure of this Spotify-UMG partnership will likely influence how other media companies approach generative AI licensing. The critical test will be whether the tool generates sufficient user demand and revenue to justify the operational and licensing overhead, or whether it remains a niche feature for superfans willing to pay extra.
items.original_title: Spotify is launching AI-generated remixes

items.headline: Google's Gemini Avatar Tool Creates Photorealistic Video Deepfakes of Users
items.body:
Google has rolled out a new feature in its Gemini app that generates video deepfakes of individual users—digital clones that can perform any action requested through natural language prompts. A tester using the feature generated two 10-second clips of himself singing to a dinosaur at Dolores Park and surfing under the Golden Gate Bridge, both powered by Google's new Omni video model. The results were photorealistic enough to be unsettling: the avatar captured his mannerisms, voice cadence, and physical appearance well enough that it felt less like watching a video of himself than like watching an alternate version of himself perform scripted actions.

## Avatar Creation and Current Constraints

Setting up a personal avatar takes approximately five minutes through the Gemini app. Users sit in well-lit conditions while their phone camera records them reading two-digit numbers and performing head movements—left and right. The feature is currently available only to Google's AI Pro subscribers, who pay $20 monthly but face strict usage limits that reset every five hours. Google has implemented a safeguard that prevents users from generating videos of other people's likenesses, unlike OpenAI's now-discontinued Sora app, which allowed users to specify whose face should appear in generated content. The clothing worn during avatar setup typically appears in subsequent generations.

## Safety Questions Amid Broader Deepfake Concerns

The timing of this launch carries weight given documented harms in the deepfake ecosystem. Nonconsensual deepfakes of women have proliferated across platforms with minimal friction, yet Google's product team—led by Nicole Brichtova at Google DeepMind—frames the feature as safety-conscious, stating the company attempts "to prevent harm" while avoiding "blocking benign things." The distinction between preventing misuse and enabling legitimate creative uses remains unresolved. For enterprises deploying generative AI systems, the release signals that photorealistic video synthesis tied to individual identities is now accessible at consumer scale, requiring organizations to consider authentication, verification, and fraud detection frameworks that account for this capability.
items.original_title: I Cloned Myself With Gemini’s AI Avatar Tool. The Result Was Unnervingly Me
